With total liabilities of $110 000 and owners' equity of just $70 000, the business is highly geared, meaning banks have considerable influence over its financial management.

Firstly, banks set the interest rate and loan covenants on the long-term loan, which directly affect cash outflows and profitability. If the Reserve Bank lifts the cash rate, the business's repayments would rise, squeezing liquidity. Secondly, banks assess the business's current ratio of 1.5:1 and gearing when deciding whether to extend additional finance. Given the high debt level, banks may refuse further lending or demand higher interest rates and security, restricting the business's ability to fund growth strategies and invest in new assets.

questions.prompt: Explain how TWO global market influences could affect the solvency of this business.
questions.stimulus: A travel agency is focused on expanding its sales in several countries.

criteria.description: Clearly explains two distinct global market influences and their effects on solvency in context.
Outlines two global influences or explains one in detail.
Identifies global influences with limited explanation.

questions.exemplar: The global economic outlook directly affects the travel agency's solvency. When the international economy is strong, demand for travel rises and the agency may borrow to fund expansion across new countries; if growth then slows, the agency is left with high debt and insufficient cash flow to meet long-term obligations, weakening solvency. Conversely, a downturn reduces sales while debt obligations remain fixed. Interest rates are a second key influence. If global interest rates rise, the cost of servicing overseas loans increases, lifting expenses and the debt-to-equity ratio — given the agency is already at 1:1, this would severely threaten solvency. Falling interest rates would reduce interest expense, ease repayment pressure and improve the agency's ability to meet long-term debts, strengthening solvency and supporting safer international expansion.

questions.prompt: A hairdresser includes proceeds of the sale of salon equipment in the business's income statement. Which limitation of financial reporting is evident?
questions.explanation: Normalised earnings refers to removing one-off, non-recurring items (like the sale of equipment) so that core profitability is shown accurately. Including a one-off sale inflates regular earnings and misrepresents ongoing performance.

questions.prompt: A business reported Sales of $275,000, Cost of goods sold of $145,000, and Expenses of $35,000 in 2020. Which of the following shows the gross profit and the net profit ratio for this business in 2020?

Using the same business data — Sales: $250,000 (2019), $275,000 (2020); COGS: $145,000 both years; Expenses: $35,000 both years — which of the following describes the change in both the gross profit ratio and expense ratio from 2019 to 2020?
options: Gross profit ratio: Improved / Expense ratio: Improved
Gross profit ratio: Improved / Expense ratio: Worsened
Gross profit ratio: Worsened / Expense ratio: Improved
Gross profit ratio: Worsened / Expense ratio: Worsened
questions.explanation: GP ratio: 2019 = 105/250 = 42%; 2020 = 130/275 = 47.3% — improved. Expense ratio: 2019 = 35/250 = 14%; 2020 = 35/275 = 12.7% — improved (lower is better). Both ratios improved.

questions.prompt: Extract from balance sheet as at June 2020: Current assets $1,700; Non-current assets $8,650; Current liabilities $1,100; Non-current liabilities $6,500. 2019 data: Debt to equity 2:1 (200%); Current ratio 1.25:1 (125%). Industry averages 2019-2020: Debt to equity 1:1 (100%); Current ratio 2:1 (200%). The liquidity position of this business in 2020 indicates that it is
options: better than the industry average and has improved since 2019.
better than the industry average and has worsened since 2019.
worse than the industry average and has improved since 2019.
worse than the industry average and has worsened since 2019.
questions.explanation: Current ratio 2020 = 1700 ÷ 1100 = 1.55:1 (155%). This is worse than industry average of 2:1 but improved from 2019's 1.25:1.

questions.prompt: Using the same balance sheet data — Current assets $1,700; Non-current assets $8,650; Current liabilities $1,100; Non-current liabilities $6,500; 2019 debt-to-equity 2:1; industry average 1:1 — the gearing for this business in 2020 indicates that it is
options: better than the industry average and has improved since 2019.
better than the industry average and has worsened since 2019.
worse than the industry average and has improved since 2019.
worse than the industry average and has worsened since 2019.
questions.explanation: Total liabilities = $7,600; Total equity = Total assets − Total liabilities = $10,350 − $7,600 = $2,750. Debt-to-equity = 7600 ÷ 2750 ≈ 2.76:1 (276%). This is worse than the industry average of 1:1 and has worsened from 2019's 2:1.

questions.prompt: Calculate the accounts receivable turnover ratio (sales ÷ accounts receivable) for this business.
questions.stimulus: Financial information for robotics company: Sales $225,000; Cost of goods sold $105,000; Selling expenses $8,500; Administration expenses $6,000; Financial expenses $4,000; Accounts receivable $30,000; Credit policy 30 days; Industry expense ratio 10%.

criteria.description: Correctly calculates the accounts receivable turnover ratio.
Mostly correct calculation with minor error.
Attempts calculation with significant error.

questions.exemplar: Accounts receivable turnover = Sales ÷ Accounts receivable = $225,000 ÷ $30,000 = 7.5 times per year. Expressed in days: 365 ÷ 7.5 = approximately 49 days.

questions.prompt: Interpret this business's expense ratio (total expenses ÷ sales).
questions.stimulus: Financial information for robotics company: Sales $225,000; Cost of goods sold $105,000; Selling expenses $8,500; Administration expenses $6,000; Financial expenses $4,000; Accounts receivable $30,000; Credit policy 30 days; Industry expense ratio 10%.

criteria.description: Correctly calculates the expense ratio and interprets it in the business context.
Correctly calculates or interprets the expense ratio.
Attempts calculation or interpretation with errors.

questions.exemplar: Total expenses = $8,500 + $6,000 + $4,000 = $18,500. Expense ratio = $18,500 ÷ $225,000 = 8.2%. This means that for every $1 of sales generated, approximately 8.2 cents is absorbed by operating expenses. This is favourable compared to the industry expense ratio of 10%, indicating the business is managing its expenses more efficiently than its competitors.

questions.prompt: Explain TWO ways in which comparative ratio analysis can be used by this business to assess its financial position.
questions.stimulus: Financial information for robotics company: Sales $225,000; Cost of goods sold $105,000; Selling expenses $8,500; Administration expenses $6,000; Financial expenses $4,000; Accounts receivable $30,000; Credit policy 30 days; Industry expense ratio 10%.

criteria.description: Clearly explains two ways comparative ratio analysis can assess the business's financial position, with relevant application.
Outlines two ways with some application, or one way in detail.
Provides general information about comparative ratio analysis.

questions.exemplar:
Comparative ratio analysis allows a business to evaluate its financial position by benchmarking ratios against meaningful reference points. Two ways the robotics company can use this analysis are:

First, comparison over different time periods (trend analysis). By calculating ratios such as the accounts receivable turnover, expense ratio, current ratio and gross profit ratio across multiple years, management can identify whether financial performance is improving or deteriorating. For example, if last year's accounts receivable turnover was 9 times but this year's is 7.5 times, this signals worsening debt collection — prompting strategies such as discounts for early payment.

Second, comparison against industry averages. The business has an expense ratio of 8.2%, which is below the industry standard of 10%, indicating superior expense management. Similar comparisons can be made for liquidity, gearing and profitability ratios to identify areas of competitive strength and weakness. This allows the robotics company to set realistic financial targets and identify where strategic changes are needed.

questions.prompt: Consider the following financial information provided by a business. Year 1: Revenue $100M, Gross Profit $80M, Net Profit $30M. Year 2: Revenue $80M, Gross Profit $60M, Net Profit $30M. Which of the following must have occurred between Year 1 and Year 2?
questions.explanation: Gross profit fell by $20M but net profit stayed the same, so expenses must have fallen by $20M to offset the drop. COGS = Revenue − Gross Profit: Year 1 = $20M, Year 2 = $20M — COGS is unchanged.

questions.prompt: Company A intends to buy Company B. Company A might pay too much for Company B if Company B's financial manager decides to
options: normalise a large one-off asset sale.
capitalise their research and development expenses.
fully disclose the nature of the receivables owed to the company.
record their buildings at historical cost although they increased in value.
questions.explanation: Capitalising R&D treats it as an asset rather than an expense, inflating profit and asset values and making the company appear more valuable. Normalising a one-off sale removes distortions. Full disclosure helps the buyer. Historical cost would understate, not overstate, value.

questions.prompt: Identify where the business could record the loan and the properties on its balance sheet.
questions.stimulus: A luxury Australian handbag business is going to expand. They intend to borrow 30 million US dollars to buy several new retail properties across Australia.

criteria.description: Correctly identifies the balance sheet classification for both the loan and the properties.
Identifies the correct classification for one item.
N/A

questions.exemplar: The $30 million US dollar loan would be recorded as a non-current liability on the balance sheet, as it is a long-term debt obligation extending beyond 12 months. The retail properties purchased would be recorded as non-current assets, as they are tangible, long-term assets used to generate income for the business.

questions.prompt: Account for the changes in this business's current and forecast profitability and gearing.
questions.stimulus: A luxury Australian handbag business is going to expand. They intend to borrow 30 million US dollars to buy several new retail properties across Australia. Financial ratios: Gross profit - Current 65%, Forecast 70%. Net profit - Current 21%, Forecast 18%. Debt to equity - Current 0.85:1 (85%), Forecast 1.5:1 (150%).

criteria.description: Provides detailed reasoning that explains why each ratio is forecast to change, integrating gearing and both profitability measures.
Provides reasons for changes in either profitability or gearing, or both with less detail.
Makes basic statements about profitability or gearing.

questions.exemplar:
The forecast changes in this business's financial ratios can be directly accounted for by the proposed $30 million expansion funded entirely through US debt.

The gearing ratio (debt to equity) is forecast to rise sharply from 85% to 150%. This is a direct consequence of taking on $30 million in additional long-term debt without a corresponding increase in equity. As debt rises while equity remains constant, the ratio mechanically increases, indicating heightened financial risk and greater reliance on borrowed funds.

Gross profit ratio is forecast to improve from 65% to 70%. The expansion into multiple new retail properties will significantly increase sales volume, allowing the business to negotiate better wholesale prices with leather suppliers and benefit from economies of scale and increased purchasing power. Although total COGS will rise in absolute terms, COGS as a percentage of sales will fall, widening the gross profit margin.

However, net profit ratio is forecast to fall from 21% to 18%, despite the improvement in gross profit. This is explained by the substantial new expenses associated with the expansion — most notably the interest expense on the $30m USD loan, plus increased depreciation on the new properties, additional wages, and rent or rates. These rising operating and finance costs outweigh the gross margin improvement, causing the net profit ratio to deteriorate.

questions.prompt:
A business provides the following financial information:

Year 1: Sales $600,000; COGS $200,000; Expenses $250,000
Year 2: Sales $700,000; COGS $200,000; Expenses $200,000
Owner's equity: $500,000
Gross profit ratio (industry average): 78%

Which of the following reflects the changes to profitability in Year 2?
options: Return on owner's equity has improved and the gross profit ratio is worse than the industry average
Return on owner's equity has worsened and the gross profit ratio is worse than the industry average
Return on owner's equity has improved and the gross profit ratio is better than the industry average
Return on owner's equity has worsened and the gross profit ratio is better than the industry average
questions.explanation: Year 1 net profit = $150,000 → ROE = 30%; Year 2 net profit = $300,000 → ROE = 60% (improved). Year 2 gross profit ratio = ($700,000 − $200,000) / $700,000 ≈ 71.4%, which is below the 78% industry average (worse).

questions.prompt: Calculate the efficiency (total expenses ÷ total sales) of this business.
questions.stimulus: A gifts and homewares business sells goods such as candles, bags, cushions, soaps and jewellery. Financial information: Operating income (revenue) $1,500,000; Gross profit $1,100,000; Expenses $600,000; Total liabilities $2,000,000; Net profit $500,000; Owner's equity $1,800,000.

criteria.description: Correctly calculates the efficiency ratio showing working.
N/A for 1-mark question.
Incorrect calculation or no working shown.

questions.exemplar: Efficiency = Total expenses ÷ Total sales = $600,000 ÷ $1,500,000 = 0.4 or 40% (expressed as a ratio: 0.4:1).

questions.prompt: Explain how the expense ratio can help this business determine their efficiency.
questions.stimulus: A gifts and homewares business sells goods such as candles, bags, cushions, soaps and jewellery. Financial information: Operating income (revenue) $1,500,000; Gross profit $1,100,000; Expenses $600,000; Total liabilities $2,000,000; Net profit $500,000; Owner's equity $1,800,000.

criteria.description: Clearly shows the relationship between the expense ratio and business efficiency with reference to the data.
Describes features of the expense ratio and efficiency with some application.
Makes a basic statement about the expense ratio or efficiency.

questions.exemplar: The expense ratio (total expenses ÷ total sales) measures how effectively a business uses its resources to generate revenue. For this gifts and homewares business, the ratio is 40%, meaning the business spends 40 cents in expenses for every dollar of sales. A lower expense ratio indicates greater efficiency, as more revenue is converted into profit. By tracking the expense ratio over time and comparing it to industry benchmarks, the business can identify whether indirect costs such as rent, utilities or administration are rising disproportionately. Reducing these expenses while maintaining or growing sales would improve efficiency and ultimately boost profitability.

questions.prompt: Why is the level of gearing an important consideration for the lender to this business?
questions.stimulus: A gifts and homewares business sells goods such as candles, bags, cushions, soaps and jewellery. Financial information: Operating income (revenue) $1,500,000; Gross profit $1,100,000; Expenses $600,000; Total liabilities $2,000,000; Net profit $500,000; Owner's equity $1,800,000. The family-owned business is considering expansion by opening two new stores. Their bank has given approval to borrow $3,000,000 at 4% interest to finance their expansion.

criteria.description: Provides a clear reason why gearing is important for the lender, with reference to the business's financial position.
Demonstrates understanding of why gearing matters to the lender.
Provides limited information about gearing.

questions.exemplar: Gearing (the debt-to-equity ratio) measures the proportion of a business's funding sourced from debt compared to equity. For lenders, gearing is critical because it indicates the level of financial risk in lending to the business. Currently, the business has total liabilities of $2,000,000 and owner's equity of $1,800,000, giving a gearing ratio just above 1:1. If the bank approves the additional $3,000,000 loan, total debt would rise to $5,000,000, significantly increasing the gearing ratio and the risk that the business could not meet repayments if cash flow declined. The lender uses gearing to assess long-term solvency and decide whether the business can reliably service the new loan plus interest.

questions.prompt: An extract from a business balance sheet shows: Cash $6 500, Accounts receivable $12 500, Inventory $40 000, Furniture and fittings $16 000, Plant machinery $12 500, Office equipment $5 000, Buildings $360 000, Total assets $452 500. Liabilities: Accounts payable $18 500, Overdraft $3 500, Short-term bank loan $21 400, Mortgage $13 000, Capital $45 000, Net profit $134 100. Which strategy would be most effective in improving the current ratio for this business? (Current ratio = current assets ÷ current liabilities)
options: Borrow money on a short-term bank loan
Take on a new mortgage to purchase buildings
Offer a discount to customers for early payment
questions.explanation: Selling non-current assets (machinery/equipment) converts them into cash, a current asset, increasing the current ratio. Borrowing short-term increases current liabilities (worsening the ratio). A new mortgage adds non-current liability. Discounts may speed receivables but reduce total current assets through lower revenue. NESA accepted both A and D as the official answer.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a cash flow statement?

criteria.description: Clearly states the purpose, referencing inflows and outflows of cash over a period.
Provides a basic purpose with limited detail.
Provides minimal relevant information about cash flow statements.

questions.exemplar: A cash flow statement identifies the inflows and outflows of cash within a business over a specific period, typically broken into operating, investing and financing activities. It allows managers to assess liquidity, ensure the business can meet short-term obligations, and identify cash management issues.

questions.prompt:
A business has the following financial information.
Sales $800 000
Cost of goods sold $200 000
Gross profit $600 000
Net profit $250 000
Expense ratio = total expenses ÷ sales
What is the expense ratio for this business?
questions.explanation: Total expenses = Gross profit − Net profit = $600 000 − $250 000 = $350 000. Expense ratio = $350 000 ÷ $800 000 = 43.75%, rounded to 44%.

questions.prompt:
The table shows the cash flow information for a business.

What is the closing cash balance for September?
A business owner is selling their business. If the business owner is unethical, what may they be motivated to do?
questions.explanation: An unethical seller would inflate income and reduce reported expenses to make the business appear more profitable and increase its sale price. The other combinations would reduce the apparent value of the business.

questions.prompt:
The following is an extract from a business balance sheet.
Assets: Cash at bank $5 000; Accounts receivable $20 000; Inventory $5 000; Office equipment $70 000; Motor vehicles $80 000.
Liabilities: Overdraft $9 000; Accounts payable $11 000; Long-term loan $90 000.

Calculate the current ratio for this business. Show all working. (Current ratio = current assets ÷ current liabilities)
questions.stimulus:
Balance sheet extract:
Assets: Cash at bank $5 000; Accounts receivable $20 000; Inventory $5 000; Office equipment $70 000; Motor vehicles $80 000.
Liabilities: Overdraft $9 000; Accounts payable $11 000; Long-term loan $90 000.

criteria.description: Correctly identifies current assets and current liabilities, shows working and arrives at the correct current ratio of 1.5:1.

questions.prompt: An extract from a cash flow document shows opening balance $2 000 in July, with cash in and cash out across July–December. Cash out exceeds cash in in September ($5 000 in, $7 000 out) and November ($8 000 in, $10 000 out). In which month should this business have adopted cash flow management strategies?
questions.explanation: NESA accepted both A and B. Strategies should be implemented before a shortfall occurs — in August (the month prior to the September deficit) so the business can manage payments and revenue ahead of negative cash flow. September is also accepted as the month the deficit first appears.

questions.prompt: In the hope of generating long-term profit, a medical company spends $2 billion developing medicine to cure a disease. This is recorded on the company's balance sheet. Which limitation of financial reports does this demonstrate?
questions.explanation: Recording R&D as an asset on the balance sheet rather than expensing it on the income statement is capitalising expenses — a recognised limitation that can overstate profit and asset values. Timing issues relate to when transactions are recorded, asset valuation concerns measurement methods, and debt repayment is not a financial report limitation.

questions.prompt: A business calculated its gearing ratio as 75% in 2023 and 66% in 2024. Which of the following is a correct statement about the business in 2024?
options: The debt to equity ratio has improved.
There is a higher risk of business failure.
The solvency of this business has declined.
The owner will have a higher return-on-equity.
questions.explanation: A lower gearing ratio means proportionally less debt relative to equity, so the debt-to-equity position has improved. Lower gearing reduces (not increases) failure risk, improves (not declines) solvency, and typically lowers return-on-equity because less leverage is used.

questions.prompt: What does the expense ratio for 2024 indicate for this business?
questions.stimulus: A travel agency is focused on expanding its sales in several countries. Financial data: Sales 2023 $600 000, 2024 $700 000; Total expenses 2023 $140 000, 2024 $210 000; Net profit 2023 $160 000, 2024 $170 000; Expense ratio 2023 23.3%, 2024 ?; Debt to equity 2023 0.6:1, 2024 1:1; Return on equity 2023 8%, 2024 8.5%. Industry averages: Expense ratio 2023 23%, 2024 20%; Debt to equity 0.6:1 both years; Return on equity 2023 11%, 2024 12%. Expense ratio = total expenses ÷ sales × 100.

criteria.description: Correctly calculates the ratio and interprets its meaning for the business in comparison to prior year and industry average.
Calculates or interprets the ratio adequately.
Provides limited or incorrect information.

questions.exemplar: Expense ratio 2024 = 210 000 ÷ 700 000 × 100 = 30%. This indicates declining efficiency: expenses have risen significantly faster than sales and the ratio is well above both the 2023 figure of 23.3% and the industry average of 20%. The business is spending too much to generate each sales dollar, which is squeezing profitability and signalling a need for tighter expense control.

questions.prompt: How can undertaking a comparative ratio analysis improve this business's financial outcome?
questions.stimulus: A travel agency is focused on expanding its sales in several countries. Financial data: Sales 2023 $600 000, 2024 $700 000; Total expenses 2023 $140 000, 2024 $210 000; Net profit 2023 $160 000, 2024 $170 000; Expense ratio 2023 23.3%, 2024 30%; Debt to equity 2023 0.6:1, 2024 1:1; Return on equity 2023 8%, 2024 8.5%. Industry averages: Expense ratio 2023 23%, 2024 20%; Debt to equity 0.6:1 both years; Return on equity 2023 11%, 2024 12%.

criteria.description: Demonstrates comprehensive understanding of comparative ratio analysis using multiple ratios from the data to suggest specific financial improvements.
Shows sound understanding using at least one ratio comparison to suggest improvements.
Provides limited information about comparative ratio analysis.

questions.exemplar: Comparative ratio analysis compares the business's ratios across years and against industry benchmarks to diagnose performance and target improvement. The expense ratio has worsened from 23.3% to 30% while the industry average has improved to 20%, indicating poor efficiency — the agency could review cost centres, renegotiate supplier contracts and minimise discretionary expenses to lift profitability. The debt-to-equity ratio has risen from 0.6:1 to 1:1, well above the industry's stable 0.6:1, showing the business has taken on excessive debt and increased solvency risk; management should reduce reliance on borrowings and consider raising equity to fund overseas expansion. Return on equity is 8.5% versus an industry average of 12%, confirming below-average returns. By acting on these comparisons, the travel agency can control costs, restore solvency and lift returns to industry levels.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an essential component of a balance sheet?
questions.explanation: A balance sheet reports assets, liabilities and owner's equity at a point in time. Expenses, sales and wages appear on the income (revenue) statement, not the balance sheet.

questions.prompt: Recommend ONE financial strategy this business could implement to improve efficiency in the collection of accounts receivable.
questions.stimulus: Financial information for robotics company: Sales $225,000; Cost of goods sold $105,000; Selling expenses $8,500; Administration expenses $6,000; Financial expenses $4,000; Accounts receivable $30,000; Credit policy 30 days; Industry expense ratio 10%.

criteria.description: Recommends one financial strategy with clear, supporting reasons relevant to improving accounts receivable efficiency.
Outlines a relevant strategy with some development.
Names a strategy with limited explanation.

questions.exemplar: I recommend offering discounts for early payment as a cash-flow management strategy. The business currently collects receivables every 49 days, well beyond its 30-day credit policy, indicating customers are paying late. By offering a small discount (e.g. 2% off if paid within 10 days), the business creates a financial incentive for customers to pay quickly. This accelerates cash inflow, improving liquidity and reducing the risk of bad debts. Combined with penalties for late payment, the strategy strengthens debt collection efficiency. The reduced accounts receivable balance also lowers the need for short-term borrowing to cover working capital shortfalls.

questions.prompt: Evaluate the effectiveness of financial management strategies used to achieve profit and growth objectives.

criteria.description: Provides a sustained, well-reasoned judgement on the effectiveness of financial management strategies in achieving profit and growth objectives, integrating relevant case studies and contemporary business issues with extensive use of business terminology.
Explains the relationship between financial management strategies and profit/growth objectives with reference to case studies, using relevant business terminology.
Limited reference to financial management strategies and objectives with basic terminology.

questions.exemplar:
Financial management strategies are central to enabling a business to achieve its profit and growth objectives. By controlling cash flow, working capital, profitability and global financial exposure, businesses translate financial decisions into long-term sustainability. However, the effectiveness of these strategies depends on their alignment with the business's circumstances and external environment. This response evaluates the effectiveness of key financial management strategies in achieving profit and growth, with reference to Qantas and other contemporary examples.

Profit objectives focus on maximising the difference between revenue and costs, typically measured through net profit, gross profit and return on equity. Growth objectives involve increasing market share, sales volume or business size, often requiring reinvestment. Financial strategies must therefore generate both short-term profit and long-term capacity.

Cash flow management is highly effective in supporting both objectives. A cash flow statement enables managers to forecast inflows and outflows and prevent liquidity crises. Strategies such as distribution of payments (timing outflows to match peak inflows), offering discounts for early payment, and factoring (selling receivables to a third party at a discount) accelerate cash inflow. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Qantas relied heavily on cash flow management to survive — deferring supplier payments, drawing on credit facilities, and selling property assets to maintain liquidity while revenue collapsed. While effective in preserving short-term solvency, aggressive use of factoring can erode profit through fees, so the strategy must be carefully balanced.

Working capital management is equally important. Controlling current assets (cash, receivables and inventories) and current liabilities (payables, loans and overdrafts) ensures the business can meet obligations while funding operations. Strategies such as leasing and sale-and-leaseback are particularly effective for growth, as they free up capital tied up in fixed assets and convert it into cash for reinvestment. Qantas's $802 million sale and leaseback of its Sydney and Melbourne airport terminals (and aircraft) is a clear example — capital was released for working capital and growth initiatives, including the Project Sunrise long-haul fleet. The strategy effectively supported growth, though it increased ongoing lease expenses, which can pressure profit margins if revenue is volatile.

Profitability management directly targets profit objectives. Cost controls — distinguishing fixed and variable costs, identifying cost centres, and pursuing expense minimisation — are highly effective. Qantas's $15 billion cost-out program announced in 2020 included 6,000 redundancies, fleet retirement of Boeing 747s, and renegotiation of supplier contracts. These measures restored profitability post-pandemic, with Qantas reporting a record $2.47 billion underlying profit in FY23. Revenue controls (aligned to marketing objectives) — including price increases on premium routes, dynamic pricing of fares, and Qantas Loyalty's expansion — increased revenue alongside cost reductions. The combined effect demonstrates that profitability management is highly effective when cost and revenue strategies operate in tandem.

However, profitability strategies can risk long-term growth. Excessive cost-cutting can damage product quality, customer service and employee morale — issues Qantas faced through illegal outsourcing of ground handlers (resulting in a High Court loss in 2023), reputational damage and customer complaints. This illustrates that short-term profit gains can undermine the brand and limit future growth if not balanced ethically and strategically.

Global financial management strategies are critical for businesses operating internationally. Exchange rate fluctuations create transaction, translation and economic risks. Hedging strategies — including forward contracts, options and swaps — lock in exchange rates and protect profit margins. Qantas hedges its US dollar fuel purchases up to 80% of expected consumption, smoothing earnings volatility. Similarly, interest rate management through fixed-rate borrowing protects against rising rates. Methods of international payment (letter of credit, bill of exchange, payment in advance, clean payment) manage credit risk in offshore transactions. Derivatives, while complex, offer powerful risk management — though misuse (as in the 2008 GFC) shows that effectiveness depends on prudent use. For multinationals such as BHP, hedging commodity prices and currency has been essential in stabilising profit during cyclical downturns.

Whilst these strategies are largely effective, several limitations must be considered. First, financial strategies cannot offset poor strategic direction — a business pursuing the wrong markets or products cannot "financial-manage" its way to growth. Second, over-reliance on debt to finance growth (high gearing) increases solvency risk. Many businesses, including Virgin Australia in 2020, collapsed due to unsustainable debt levels exposed by external shocks. Third, financial management is reactive to broader economic conditions — exchange rates, interest rates and consumer confidence are outside management's control. Fourth, ethical considerations matter: pursuing profit through tax minimisation or aggressive accounting (e.g. capitalising expenses to overstate profit, as alleged in the Tesco accounting scandal) can damage reputation and trigger regulatory penalties.

In evaluation, financial management strategies are highly effective in achieving profit and growth objectives when applied in an integrated, balanced and ethical manner. Cash flow and working capital management secure short-term solvency and free up capital for reinvestment. Profitability management directly drives net profit, while global financial management protects margins from international exposure. The Qantas case demonstrates that effective combinations of these strategies can return a business to record profit even after severe disruption — but also that single-minded pursuit of profit at the expense of stakeholders can undermine long-term growth. Effectiveness, ultimately, is contingent on alignment with the business's broader strategic objectives, sound implementation, and recognition that financial strategies are tools to enable strategy, not substitutes for it. Used wisely, they remain indispensable to achieving both profit and growth.

questions.explanation: Sale and leaseback converts a fixed asset into cash (a current asset), boosting working capital available for day-to-day operations. Liquidity and cash flow would improve, not reduce, and profitability is not the primary aim.

questions.prompt: Write a business report in which you: outline some of the challenges including the global factors of costs, skills and supply the business may need to consider in recruiting and retaining staff; compare the use of debt and equity as sources of finance; recommend ONE source of finance this business could use to fund its expansion.
questions.stimulus: Future Century Gaming Pty Ltd operates an online gaming platform targeting players aged 18−40 in Australia. It has been in operation since 2012, providing subscription access to a variety of online games. The business is considering expanding into emerging global markets but is finding it challenging to recruit and retain skilled, qualified game designers and developers in Australia. It is considering international labour markets to overcome staff shortages. The business has decided to finance its expansion into global markets through external sources.

criteria.description: Comprehensive business report integrating thorough analysis of global HR factors (costs, skills, supply), detailed comparison of debt and equity finance, and a well-justified recommendation, applied to Future Century Gaming with extensive use of business terminology.
Sound business report with adequate coverage of global HR factors and finance comparison, including a recommendation, applied to the scenario with appropriate terminology.
Limited reference to HR strategies and finance, with basic terminology and minimal scenario application.

questions.exemplar:
BUSINESS REPORT: STRATEGIC EXPANSION RECOMMENDATIONS
TO: Board of Directors, Future Century Gaming Pty Ltd
FROM: Strategic Advisor
RE: Global Expansion — HR Challenges and Finance Recommendations

1.0 INTRODUCTION
This report addresses the strategic challenges facing Future Century Gaming as it expands into emerging global markets. It examines global HR considerations relating to costs, skills and supply, compares debt and equity finance, and recommends one source of finance to fund the expansion.

2.0 GLOBAL HR CHALLENGES IN RECRUITING AND RETAINING STAFF

2.1 Costs
Labour costs vary substantially across global markets. While Australian developers command salaries averaging $110 000-$150 000, equivalent talent in emerging markets such as India, Vietnam and the Philippines may cost 40-60% less. However, beyond base wages, Future Century Gaming must factor in relocation costs, visa sponsorship, training in proprietary systems, and ongoing remote work infrastructure. Currency fluctuations also affect long-term cost predictability. Companies such as Atlassian have successfully tapped global labour pools while maintaining quality, providing a useful benchmark.

2.2 Skills
The skills shortage in game design and development is a global challenge intensified by competition from major studios such as EA, Riot Games and Ubisoft. Future Century Gaming must assess whether international candidates have specialised skills in areas like Unity, Unreal Engine, multiplayer architecture and AI integration. Cultural differences in design aesthetics — particularly between Western and Asian gaming markets — also necessitate hiring staff with local market understanding. Continuous professional development and clear career pathways become critical for retention in this competitive global talent market.

2.3 Supply
The global supply of qualified game developers is constrained but geographically uneven. Emerging tech hubs such as Bangalore, Ho Chi Minh City and Warsaw offer growing talent pools. However, Future Century Gaming will compete with established multinationals offering attractive packages. Retention strategies including flexible work arrangements, performance bonuses, equity participation and a strong organisational culture are essential. Time-zone management for distributed teams further complicates collaboration and may impact productivity and team cohesion.

3.0 COMPARISON OF DEBT AND EQUITY FINANCE

3.1 Debt Finance
Debt finance involves borrowing money that must be repaid with interest, typically through mortgages, debentures, unsecured notes or commercial bills. Advantages include retention of ownership and control, tax-deductible interest payments, and a defined repayment schedule. However, debt increases the gearing ratio, requires regular repayments regardless of profitability, and may demand collateral. For Future Century Gaming, debt would create cash flow pressure during the uncertain expansion period.

3.2 Equity Finance
Equity finance involves raising capital by issuing shares, including new share issues, rights issues, placements, share purchase plans or attracting private equity investors. Advantages include no repayment obligation, no interest expense, and access to investor expertise and networks. Disadvantages include dilution of ownership, loss of control, dividend expectations, and the complex regulatory process of issuing shares. As a private company (Pty Ltd), Future Century Gaming would likely pursue private equity rather than a public listing.

3.3 Key Differences
Debt is cheaper but riskier in volatile industries; equity is more expensive in the long run but reduces financial risk. The gaming industry is high-risk and capital-intensive, with uncertain revenue projections from new markets, favouring equity. However, equity surrenders strategic flexibility — private equity firms often demand board seats and exit timelines.

4.0 RECOMMENDATION

It is recommended that Future Century Gaming pursue PRIVATE EQUITY as its primary external source of finance to fund global expansion.

Rationale:
• The company is a Pty Ltd, making private equity more accessible than a public share issue.
• Private equity investors bring not only capital but also industry expertise, international networks and operational experience — invaluable for entering unfamiliar emerging markets.
• The high-risk nature of global expansion is unsuitable for debt servicing during the cash-negative early years.
• Successful Australian tech firms such as Canva and Atlassian leveraged private equity (and venture capital) to fuel international growth before pursuing IPOs.
• Private equity also provides patient capital, allowing 3-7 year horizons aligned with global market entry timelines.

While some ownership dilution is required, this is offset by access to growth capital, strategic guidance and the ability to scale without crippling debt repayments.

5.0 CONCLUSION
Future Century Gaming faces significant but manageable HR and finance challenges. By carefully accessing global talent markets and securing private equity investment, the business is well-positioned to successfully expand into emerging markets and achieve sustainable long-term growth.

questions.prompt: The table shows extracts from the financial statements of a business for 2022: Sales $300 000, Cost of goods sold $200 000, Gross profit $100 000, Expenses $40 000. What is the net profit ratio for this business in 2022? (Net profit ratio = net profit ÷ sales)

questions.prompt: Explain a possible conflict between ONE short-term and ONE long-term financial objective.

criteria.description: Clearly explains a conflict between specified short-term and long-term financial objectives with appropriate reasoning.
Outlines a conflict with some explanation of one or both objectives.
Provides basic information about financial objectives.

questions.exemplar: A conflict can arise between the short-term objective of profitability and the long-term objective of growth. To pursue growth, a business such as Qantas may invest heavily in new aircraft, route expansion or technology upgrades, which significantly reduces short-term profits due to capital expenditure and increased depreciation. While this lowers immediate profitability and may concern shareholders, it positions the business for higher long-term revenue and market share. Managers must therefore balance these competing objectives through clear strategic planning and stakeholder communication.

questions.prompt: Explain TWO limitations of financial reports.

criteria.description: Provides a detailed explanation of TWO limitations of financial reports with examples or reasoning.
Provides a sound explanation of two limitations or detailed explanation of one.
Demonstrates basic understanding of limitations.

questions.exemplar:
Financial reports, while essential, have significant limitations that can distort the true financial position of a business.

Firstly, valuing assets presents major challenges, particularly for non-current assets such as land, buildings and intellectual property. Asset valuation is inherently subjective and dependent on market conditions, historical cost methods or accounting estimates. For example, a property purchased in 2005 may be recorded at its original cost on the balance sheet, vastly understating its current market value. Conversely, intangible assets like brand reputation and goodwill are difficult to quantify accurately, meaning stakeholders may not have a true picture of the business's worth, affecting investment and lending decisions.

Secondly, capitalising expenses is a limitation where businesses record what would normally be an expense as an asset on the balance sheet. For instance, a business may capitalise research and development costs rather than expensing them in the income statement, artificially inflating profits and asset values. This was central to corporate collapses such as WorldCom, where billions of dollars in operating expenses were improperly capitalised, misleading investors about the company's real profitability. This practice can deceive stakeholders, leading to poor financial decisions based on overstated earnings and an inflated balance sheet.

questions.prompt: An export company based in Sydney sells Australian-made art to the Asian market. Which form of payment has the lowest level of risk for the exporter?
questions.explanation: Payment in advance carries the lowest risk for the exporter because money is received before goods are shipped. Clean payment is risky as goods are sent before payment. Letter of credit reduces risk but isn't zero. Bills of exchange carry payment-default risk.

questions.prompt: Explain how offering discounts for early payment may improve a business's cash flow and reduce working capital.

criteria.description: Explains clearly how early payment discounts affect both cash flow and working capital, including the trade-offs involved.
Demonstrates sound understanding of how discounts affect cash flow or working capital.
Provides basic information about discounts or working capital.

questions.exemplar:
Offering discounts for early payment is a cash flow management strategy designed to encourage debtors to pay before the standard credit term, typically 30 days, in exchange for a small reduction in the amount owed. This converts accounts receivable into cash faster, improving short-term cash inflows and giving the business immediate funds to meet operating expenses, supplier payments and unexpected costs.

While cash flow improves, the discount reduces total revenue received. This decreases current assets (cash and receivables combined fall by the discount amount), which in turn reduces working capital — the difference between current assets and current liabilities. Therefore, the business gains liquidity and reduces the risk of bad debts but sacrifices some profitability and the size of its working capital. The strategy is most effective when the cost of the discount is less than the cost of alternative finance, such as bank overdraft interest.

questions.prompt: How can financial strategies affect the objectives of financial management?

criteria.description: Demonstrates comprehensive understanding of how financial strategies affect financial management objectives, integrates relevant case studies and contemporary issues, and presents a sustained, logical response using accurate business terminology.
Demonstrates sound understanding of how financial strategies affect objectives, with reference to case studies and contemporary issues.
Describes some aspects of financial strategies or objectives with limited reference to case studies.

questions.exemplar:
Financial management involves planning, monitoring and controlling a business's financial resources to achieve its strategic objectives of profitability, growth, efficiency, liquidity and solvency, in both the short and long term. Financial strategies — including cash flow management, working capital management, profitability management and global financial management — are the tools managers use to pursue these objectives. The relationship between strategies and objectives is interdependent, as strategies designed to improve one objective often involve trade-offs against another. This response examines how each major financial strategy affects these objectives, drawing on case studies including Qantas and Apple.

Cash flow management strategies directly affect liquidity and, indirectly, profitability. A cash flow statement allows managers to forecast inflows and outflows and identify potential shortfalls. The distribution of payments strategy spreads cash outflows evenly across the period to avoid liquidity crises, while discounts for early payment accelerate receivables collection, boosting cash on hand. Factoring sells receivables to a third party for immediate cash, again improving liquidity but at the cost of profitability because the business receives less than the full invoice value. Qantas, during the COVID-19 pandemic, used aggressive cash flow strategies including deferring payments, drawing on credit lines and raising $1.9 billion in equity to maintain liquidity when revenue collapsed. While this protected solvency, it temporarily harmed profitability and growth. This illustrates how cash flow strategies prioritise short-term liquidity, sometimes at the expense of profit and long-term growth.

Working capital management strategies control current assets and current liabilities to optimise both liquidity and efficiency. Controlling current assets involves managing cash (avoiding idle balances), receivables (tightening credit terms) and inventories (using JIT systems). Controlling current liabilities involves negotiating extended payment terms with suppliers and using overdrafts strategically. Strategies such as leasing free up working capital because the business pays periodically rather than purchasing assets outright, while sale and lease back releases capital tied up in fixed assets. Apple is renowned for negative working capital — collecting payment from customers before paying suppliers — which has supported its profitability and funded growth without external debt. However, overly tight working capital management can compromise liquidity if unexpected costs arise, and excessive leasing increases long-term expenses, affecting profitability. Effective working capital strategy therefore balances liquidity, efficiency and profitability objectives.

Profitability management strategies focus on controlling costs and revenues to maximise profit. Cost controls distinguish between fixed costs (rent, salaries) and variable costs (raw materials), use cost centres to allocate expenses to responsible managers, and pursue expense minimisation. Revenue controls align marketing objectives, pricing and sales mix with profitability targets. Aldi exemplifies cost-control strategies through its limited product range, low staffing model and minimal store fit-outs, enabling it to undercut competitors while maintaining strong profit margins and steadily growing its market share in Australia. Conversely, excessive cost cutting may damage product quality, customer satisfaction and long-term growth, demonstrating that profitability strategies must be balanced against growth and brand equity objectives.

Global financial management strategies are crucial for businesses operating internationally, addressing exchange rates, interest rates and methods of international payment. Exchange rate fluctuations directly affect profitability — a falling Australian dollar increases import costs but boosts export revenue. Hedging through forward exchange contracts locks in exchange rates, protecting profitability and improving the predictability of cash flow. Derivatives such as options provide similar protection with flexibility. Methods of international payment including payment in advance, letter of credit, clean payment and bill of exchange differ in risk and impact on liquidity. Payment in advance maximises cash flow but may deter customers, while clean payment is customer-friendly but increases risk of bad debts. BHP uses extensive hedging and derivative strategies to manage commodity price and currency volatility, stabilising profitability and protecting shareholder returns. Without such strategies, global firms would face severe earnings volatility, threatening both profitability and solvency.

A contemporary issue shaping all these strategies is the high interest rate environment of 2023–2024, with the RBA cash rate at 4.35%. Higher borrowing costs make working capital strategies such as leasing and overdrafts more expensive, eroding profitability. Many Australian businesses have responded with stricter cash flow management — chasing receivables faster and tightening inventory — to maintain liquidity and solvency. This demonstrates how the macroeconomic environment forces businesses to recalibrate the trade-offs between objectives.

Another contemporary issue is ethical financial management. Strategies must comply with accounting standards and avoid practices such as understating expenses or overstating revenue, which artificially boost profitability but damage long-term solvency and reputation, as seen in collapses such as Dick Smith Electronics. Ethical financial reporting builds investor confidence, supporting long-term growth.

In conclusion, financial strategies are the operational levers through which financial management objectives are achieved. Cash flow strategies primarily target liquidity, working capital management balances liquidity and efficiency, profitability management drives profit and supports growth, and global financial management protects profitability and solvency from international risks. However, the objectives are interdependent and often in tension: improving liquidity through factoring reduces profitability; cutting costs may impede growth; aggressive expansion threatens solvency. Effective financial managers, like those at Qantas, Apple, Aldi and BHP, integrate multiple strategies, monitor performance through financial ratios, and adapt to contemporary influences such as interest rates and ethical expectations to achieve a balanced pursuit of short- and long-term financial objectives.

questions.prompt: Explain how financial strategies can achieve liquidity and profitability objectives.

criteria.description: Comprehensive explanation of multiple financial strategies and how each achieves liquidity and profitability objectives, with case study application and contemporary business issues, using sustained terminology.
Sound explanation covering several financial strategies and their links to liquidity and/or profitability with case study reference.
Limited reference to finance with basic terminology.

questions.exemplar:
Financial management is the planning, organising and controlling of a business's financial resources to achieve its objectives. Two of the most important objectives are liquidity — the ability to meet short-term debts as they fall due — and profitability — the ability to maximise returns relative to costs and capital invested. Businesses use cash flow management, working capital management, profitability management and global financial management strategies to achieve both objectives simultaneously, although tensions sometimes arise between them. The following explanation shows how each strategy contributes to liquidity and profitability, with reference to businesses such as Qantas, Woolworths and Apple.

Cash flow management is critical to liquidity because it ensures that cash inflows are timed to meet cash outflows. A cash flow statement identifies surpluses and shortfalls so managers can act early. The distribution of payments strategy spreads outflows evenly across the period to avoid cash crunches — for example, staggering supplier payments and rent. Discounts for early payment, such as 2/10 net 30, accelerate the collection of accounts receivable, bringing cash in faster and lifting liquidity. Factoring, where receivables are sold to a third party for an immediate (discounted) cash payment, instantly improves liquidity, though the discount reduces profitability slightly. Qantas used aggressive cash flow management strategies during the COVID-19 pandemic — deferring payments, raising capital and renegotiating creditor terms — to maintain liquidity when revenues collapsed. Effective cash flow management therefore secures short-term survival (liquidity) while protecting long-term earnings (profitability) by avoiding insolvency, late fees and reputational damage.

Working capital management focuses on the control of current assets (cash, receivables, inventories) and current liabilities (payables, loans, overdrafts) to ensure liquidity while supporting profitability. Holding sufficient cash and accessible reserves meets day-to-day obligations, supporting liquidity. Tighter credit policies and active receivables management reduce bad debts and speed up collections, improving both liquidity and profitability. Inventory control — using just-in-time systems, as Apple does with its tightly synchronised global supply chain — frees up cash tied up in stock and reduces storage and obsolescence costs, lifting profitability while maintaining product availability. On the liabilities side, negotiating longer payable terms with suppliers preserves cash, while overdrafts and short-term loans provide a flexible liquidity buffer. Strategies such as leasing and sale-and-leaseback convert fixed assets into cash without losing operational use — Qantas and many retailers have used sale-and-leaseback of aircraft and property to release cash, boosting liquidity, although lease payments slightly reduce long-term profitability. Skilful working capital management therefore directly serves liquidity and indirectly supports profitability.

Profitability management uses cost controls and revenue controls to maximise returns. Cost controls include managing fixed and variable costs, using cost centres to assign accountability, and expense minimisation strategies such as energy efficiency, renegotiating contracts and outsourcing non-core activities. Woolworths, for example, continuously reviews supplier contracts and centralises distribution to lower variable costs per unit, lifting profit margins. Revenue controls align with marketing objectives — setting prices, targeting profitable customer segments and developing higher-margin product lines. Apple's premium pricing strategy is a powerful revenue control: by targeting customers willing to pay a premium for design and ecosystem, Apple sustains gross margins around 40% and generates very high profitability. Importantly, profitability strategies must not undermine liquidity: aggressive cost cutting that delays supplier payments too far, or chasing high-margin sales on long credit terms, can starve the business of cash. The best businesses pair cost discipline with strong receivables control so profitability gains also strengthen liquidity.

Global financial management strategies are increasingly relevant for businesses operating internationally and affect both objectives. Exchange rates influence both the cost of imports and the revenue from exports — an unfavourable movement can erode profitability. Hedging strategies such as forward contracts, options and swaps lock in exchange rates so that future cash flows are predictable, protecting liquidity and profitability simultaneously. Qantas uses hedging on fuel and currency to smooth costs, preventing sudden cash outflows that would damage liquidity. Interest rate management — choosing between fixed and variable rate borrowing, or using interest rate swaps — controls finance costs, supporting profitability. Choosing appropriate methods of international payment also matters: payment in advance maximises liquidity but may deter customers, while letters of credit balance risk and predictability, and clean payment or bill of exchange offer different trade-offs between liquidity risk and customer relationships. Derivatives, while powerful, must be used carefully — speculative use can threaten both objectives, as seen in past corporate collapses.

There are inevitable tensions between liquidity and profitability. Holding large cash balances supports liquidity but earns low returns, reducing profitability; investing surplus cash in productive assets boosts profitability but reduces immediate liquidity. Offering generous credit terms attracts customers and revenue (profitability) but extends receivables and weakens liquidity. Skilful financial management balances the two — for example, by combining a small high-interest cash reserve, disciplined receivables collection and selective investment in growth assets.

Contemporary issues such as rising interest rates, supply chain disruption, climate-related financial disclosures and the demand for sustainability investment make these strategies more important than ever. Businesses that integrate cash flow management, working capital management, profitability management and global financial management strategies — as demonstrated by Qantas, Woolworths and Apple — can achieve both liquidity and profitability objectives, ensuring short-term survival and long-term success.

questions.prompt: A NSW employer develops a return to work plan in consultation with an insurer and an injured employee. Which legislation requires the employer to undertake this action?
questions.explanation: The Workers Compensation Act 1987 (NSW) requires employers to develop return-to-work plans for injured employees. The WHS Act focuses on preventing injury, the Fair Work Act covers general employment conditions, and the AHRC Act addresses discrimination.

questions.prompt: Betty believes she is eligible to ask her employer for flexible working arrangements. Which of the following is a provision of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) that gives Betty the right to make this request?
questions.explanation: The right to request flexible working arrangements is one of the National Employment Standards (minimum employment standards) under the Fair Work Act 2009. Modern awards and enterprise agreements are separate instruments, and a common law contract is an individual arrangement.

questions.prompt: Explain how technological influences and globalisation can lead to a company reducing staff numbers. Support your answer with relevant examples.

criteria.description: Clearly explains both how technological influences and globalisation can lead to staff reductions, supported by relevant business examples.
Provides characteristics and features of how technology and/or globalisation lead to staff reductions, with at least one example.
Identifies basic features of technology or globalisation with limited link to staff reductions.

questions.exemplar:
Technological influences refer to advances in equipment, software and automation that change how businesses operate. As technology improves productivity, it often replaces human labour. For example, Amazon's introduction of robotic warehouse systems and automated sorting has reduced its need for manual pickers, while Australian banks like CBA have closed branches and reduced teller numbers as digital banking apps replace face-to-face transactions.

Globalisation refers to the increased economic integration between nations, enabling businesses to access overseas markets, labour and resources. This often leads to offshoring of production to countries with lower labour costs, causing local retrenchments. For example, Bonds (Pacific Brands) moved its manufacturing offshore to China in 2009, resulting in approximately 1,800 Australian job losses. Similarly, Holden ceased Australian manufacturing in 2017, partly because it could not compete with cheaper global production.

Together, technology and globalisation reduce the demand for domestic labour, leading to retrenchments and workforce restructuring.

questions.prompt: At which stage of the economic cycle is it most likely for a business to begin to reduce staff working hours?
questions.explanation: A downswing is the early stage of contraction when demand begins to fall — businesses respond by cutting hours before resorting to retrenchments. By recession (C), staff are more likely to be terminated. Boom and upswing involve growing demand.

questions.prompt: Outline how corporate culture can affect staff turnover.

criteria.description: Clearly outlines the relationship between corporate culture and staff turnover.
Provides relevant information about either corporate culture or staff turnover.
N/A

questions.exemplar: Corporate culture refers to the shared values, beliefs, attitudes and behaviours of employees within a business. A positive corporate culture — characterised by mutual respect, recognition and open communication — makes employees feel valued and engaged, reducing voluntary staff turnover. Conversely, a toxic or negative culture creates dissatisfaction and disengagement, leading to higher rates of resignation and increased turnover costs.

questions.prompt: Discuss the possible implications of these influences on human resource management.
questions.stimulus: In recent times governments have imposed restrictions on businesses such as lockdowns, social distancing and the movement of people. As a result, businesses have changed the way their employees work and communicate with each other.

criteria.description: Provides detailed discussion of advantages and disadvantages of these influences on multiple aspects of human resource management.
Identifies features of HR management and the implications of these influences for business.
Provides limited information about HR management.

questions.exemplar:
Government-imposed restrictions such as lockdowns and social distancing have created significant implications across all four key processes of human resource management — acquisition, development, maintenance and separation.

In terms of acquisition, reduced consumer demand for many businesses (particularly retail and hospitality) has resulted in hiring freezes and reduced recruitment. However, businesses able to operate remotely have benefited from a wider talent pool, recruiting skilled workers from interstate or overseas at potentially lower cost. For example, many Australian tech businesses such as Atlassian have permanently embraced remote-first hiring.

Development has also shifted significantly. Traditional face-to-face training has largely been replaced by online learning platforms and virtual workshops. While this lowers travel and venue costs, it can reduce engagement and the effectiveness of on-the-job training, particularly for new recruits requiring socialisation into corporate culture.

Maintenance is perhaps the most heavily affected area. Work health and safety obligations have extended into employees' homes, requiring businesses to ensure home offices are ergonomic and that employee mental health is supported. Communication has shifted to digital platforms like Microsoft Teams and Zoom, raising new ethical concerns around online conduct, surveillance and the blurring of work-life boundaries. Maintaining corporate culture and team cohesion in remote environments presents an ongoing challenge.

Finally, separations have increased in industries unable to operate during restrictions, with redundancies and stand-downs common. This raises legal and ethical responsibilities to provide fair redundancy payments and outplacement support.

Overall, while these influences have presented challenges including increased compliance burdens and cultural fragmentation, they have also created opportunities for HR managers to embrace flexible working, reduce costs and access a broader, more diverse workforce, fundamentally reshaping HRM practice into the future.

questions.prompt: Outline how an increase in the minimum wage rate could affect business.

questions.prompt: Outline how the minimum employment standards protect employees in Australia. Use examples in your answer.

questions.exemplar: The National Employment Standards (NES) under the Fair Work Act 2009 set out 11 minimum entitlements that apply to all employees in Australia, regardless of their employment contract or award. These standards provide a safety net to protect basic employee rights. For example, full-time employees are entitled to four weeks of paid annual leave each year and a paid day off on public holidays. Employees are also entitled to a maximum of 38 ordinary hours of work per week, paid personal/carer's leave, and parental leave. These protections ensure fair treatment and prevent employer exploitation.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is involved in making workplace agreements and resolving workplace disputes?

Unions advocate but don't legislate. Society influences but doesn't implement. Employer associations represent business interests but lack legislative power.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO) legislation?

questions.exemplar: Equal Employment Opportunity legislation ensures that all individuals are treated fairly in the workplace, with employment decisions based on merit rather than personal characteristics such as gender, age, ethnicity, religion or disability. questions.prompt: Outline TWO human resource issues found in this advertisement.
questions.stimulus: Advertisement for Teddy Bear Store (Est. 1990) seeking a Store Manager. Role: Manage a team of 8 staff members, manage day-to-day operations. Responsibilities: Organise inventory, train staff, assist customers. General information: $65 000 salary, Union membership not permitted, No superannuation included.

Secondly, prohibiting union membership contravenes the Fair Work Act 2009, which protects employees' right to freedom of association, including the right to join a union without discrimination from the employer.
